one_line_takeaway: A conceptual five-level (Operator/Collaborator/Consultant/Approver/Observer) framework that treats agent autonomy as a design decision separable from capability, plus two proposed-but-unevaluated governance/evaluation mechanisms (autonomy certificates and assisted evaluation).
key_finding: The central claim is that autonomy is separable from capability: a highly capable agent can be designed to operate at low autonomy (e.g., consulting the user before each action) and a less capable agent can be fully autonomous on well-scoped tasks. The paper argues that because current agent evaluation is dominated by capability benchmarks (task-completion accuracy), autonomy is under-measured, and that governance frameworks which use binary 'can the agent autonomously complete task X' thresholds (e.g., Anthropic RSP, DeepMind Frontier Safety Framework) are too coarse -- an L5 agent that generates revenue autonomously and an L4 agent that does so with one user approval pose similar real-world risk. Autonomy certificates and assisted evaluations are proposed to close this gap.
core_contribution: A conceptual position essay (Knight First Amendment Institute web publication, cs.HC) arguing that an AI agent's level of autonomy should be treated as a deliberate design decision, decoupled from the agent's capability and operational environment. Its three own contributions are: (1) a user-centered five-level autonomy framework (Table 1) organized around the role a user takes when interacting with an agent -- L1 Operator, L2 Collaborator, L3 Consultant, L4 Approver, L5 Observer -- with per-level must-have controls, characteristics, and open design questions; (2) 'autonomy certificates', a proposed governance mechanism in which a third-party governing body issues a digital certificate attesting the maximum autonomy level at which an agent may operate, backed by an 'autonomy case' analogous to a safety case; and (3) 'assisted evaluation', a proposed evaluation setup that measures the minimum level of user involvement an agent needs to exceed a task-completion threshold T, thereby measuring autonomy separately from capability. No new system is built and no experiments are run; all three contributions are conceptual proposals illustrated with a single running example (a computer-using agent answering 'the economic impact of generative AI in the US since ChatGPT').
threat_model: The paper presents no formal threat model and runs no attack/defense experiments; it is a governance and HCI framework. It does, however, raise several security/safety risks as motivation, attributed here as the paper's own framing: (1) attack surface grows as autonomy increases (citing Li et al. [23], on commercial LLM agents being vulnerable to simple attacks); (2) L4 'Approver' agents introduce heightened security concerns from storing sensitive user credentials such as API keys and passwords; (3) misaligned agents could exploit user disengagement / 'rubber-stamping' at L4 to gradually convince disengaged users to approve risky actions and thereby escalate their effective autonomy; and (4) accountability for agent actions becomes harder to trace as autonomy rises. These are stated as open questions and motivating concerns, not as evaluated threats.
limitations: This is a non-empirical essay: no agent is built, no certificate is issued, no assisted evaluation is run, so none of the three contributions are validated. The autonomy certificate scheme leaves major practical questions open (who the governing bodies are, how 'autonomy cases' are rigorously verified, cost, gaming/misclassification, jurisdiction). The assisted-evaluation procedure is acknowledged to be potentially expensive (up to five rounds of task completion to label an agent L1). The five levels are boundary-fuzzy (the authors themselves classify OpenAI's Operator product as L2 while their L1 is also 'operator'-named, and note distinguishing consequential vs non-consequential actions is hard). Level assignments for named systems (Table 1) are the authors' judgments as of June 2025, not measured. The security discussion is motivational and not backed by any experiment.
